2: Milk production is associated with environmental impacts such as acidification and eutrophication of soil and water bodies or global warming caused by the emission of greenhouse gases (De Vries and Boer, 2010). Interest in the development of methods in order to better understand such possible environmental impacts associated with the production and consumption of products has

increased recently (ISO, 2006). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a method developed in order to assess the environmental aspects and potential environmental impacts throughout a product's life cycle and provides a widespread insight into the environmental burdens associated with a product or a human activity (ISO, 2006). Some studies have already applied LCA on typical regional (Hospido et al., 2003) or national (Chen et al., 2005; Basset-Mens et al., 2009) milk production systems or focussed on comparisons of conven- tional with organic production systems (Thomassen et al., 2008; Boer, 2003) or different production scenarios (Basset-Mens et al., 2009; Casey and Holden, 2005). No LCA on milk production has so far been elaborated for a tropical region.

Milk production in Peru has grown quickly during the last 15 years (up to 60% between 1990 and 2003; (FAOSTAT, 2008)) and the sale of milk is an important source of income especially for small- holder farmers. Most of the studies mentioned above have identi- fied the production and use of feed to be the main contributor to the total environmental impact of milk. Furthermore the produc- tion level significantly influences the environmental performance if the impact is expressed per unit of milk produced (Thomassen et al., 2009). Dairy feed and milk production levels vary consider- ably between the smallholder milk production systems in the Peruvian highlands and the coastal region. In the Peruvian high- lands, smallholder milk production is based on local Criollo cattle on permanent pastures supplemented with ryegrass-clover (Bartl et al., 2009) whereas dairy cows at the coast are Holstein-Friesian breed and are fed a diet consisting of fodder maize and concentrate (Gómez, 2008).

The hypothesis tested in this study was that typical Peruvian smallholder dairy systems in the Andean highlands and at the coast have different potential environmental impacts mainly due to different feeding systems and milk production levels.

4: In this project we applied an attributional LCA (Rebitzer et al., 2004), which analyses the resource inputs and the emissions within a defined system attributed to the production of 1 kg energy corrected milk (ECM). According to ISO 14044 (ISO, 2006) the study includes a goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation of results.
5: The objective of this study was to examine the life cycle of milk produced in two typical Peruvian smallholder dairy systems in order to quantify and compare the potential environmental impacts. The main focus was put on dairy feed production as the two systems studied differ especially in the feed used. The identi- fication of the activities with the major environmental impacts will allow the establishment of specific improvement actions for each of the systems. The results are intended to be used as a baseline for further investigations or implementation studies.

1: All impact categories were expressed for the functional units of 1 kg ECM and 1 animal. For conversion of kg milk to kg ECM the yield was adjusted for energy to a standard milk with 4% fat, 3.2% protein and 4.8% lactose using the formula ECM (kg/day) = milk (kg/day) X [0.038 X fat (g/kg) + 0.024 protein (g/kg) + 0.017 lactose (g/kg)]/3.14 (ALP, 2006). Afterwards, in order to account for the different duration of lactation in the two systems, the ECM yield per lactation was allocated to a whole year. Environmental impacts were then calculated for the fraction of a cow necessary to produce 1 kg ECM (0.058% of a cow at the coast, 0.58% of a cow in the highlands). For the functional unit of 1 animal an average body weight, based on the numbers and body weights of all animals in the herd, of 299 kg for the highlands and 367 kg for the coast were assumed. Additionally, the impact categories acidification and eutrophication, which do also have a regional context, were expressed on a per hectare basis.
2: The core element of the study was to properly describe feed production. Thus the use of land, irrigation water, energy, fertilizers,
Fig. 1. System boundary for the highland production system.

pesticides and machinery for on and off farm production and pro- cessing of feed as well as transport of production inputs, crops and feed produced were included. This comprised also the production of inputs such as fuel, electricity, fertilizers and pesticides. Further- more, other inputs for the dairy herd (including lactating and dry cows, heifers, calves and bulls) such as drinking water and land for the dry lots were taken into account (Figs. 1 and 2). Both systems also included a horse and a donkey as working animals. The consumption of feed by these animals was considered as well as N emissions via feces and urine on pastures.

The equipment of the farms with roads, irrigation infrastructure, buildings and machinery was excluded as its contribution to the impacts investigated (global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication) was assumed to be low. The infrastructure of the farms in general is very basic with unfortified roads which only reach the farm gate at the coast and the main square of the village in the highlands, no on farm roads exist. Irrigation channels are unfortified and the irrigation system is based on gravity. Livestock is kept in a fenced dry lot at the coast and without any built infrastructure in the highlands and no machinery for feed production is used at the coast whereas in the highlands two communities with about 260 smallholder farms share one tractor. All maintenance operations of infrastructure are manual. Further- more, medicines and seeds were excluded due to their small impact (Cederberg, 1998). Carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from livestock respiration were not taken into account according to the Kyoto Protocol which does not consider livestock respiration to be a net source for global warming (LEAD FAO, 2006).

4: Regional data was used whenever available as the demonstra- tion of the differences between the two systems analyzed was one of the goals of this study. Data for the highland system was collected in a survey of 26 farmers from two highland communities in the central Peruvian Andes (Chalhuas and Sallahuachac; prov- ince of Jauja, district of Sincos; published in (Bartl et al., 2009); Tables 1 and 2). For its use in the LCA annual average values were calculated. Additionally the nutrient contents of 74 samples of the most common feed were available for the highland system (Bartl et al., 2009a). Another source of data with a high regional context was information generated in a feeding trial with cattle and feed typical for the central Peruvian Andes (Bartl et al., 2009b). The production and nutritional values of forages were adopted from a growing experiment conducted in Chalhuas and Sallahuachac (Bartl et al., 2009c).

For the coastal system farm level data was obtained from three surveys of farmers in the irrigation zone San Felipe, at the coast about 135 km north of Lima (province of Huaura, district of Lima). Data concerning feeding and milk production was collected from 51 member farmers of an association of smallholder dairy producers
Fig. 2. System boundary for the coastal production system.

for the preparation of a baseline document for rural extension activities by the Universidad Nacional Agraria La Molina in Lima, Faculty of Animal Production (Gómez, 2008) (Tables 1 and 2). Another 20 farmers of the same association were interviewed twice

Data not included in these reports was collected in detailed interviews with 3 member farmers and also samples of concentrate ingredients were taken. Production inputs for the off farm cultivation and processing of the maize straw and concentrate ingredients was obtained from literature (see footnotes to Table 2). For wheat production in Argentina no information about production inputs and activities was available and due to similar yields data for conventional wheat in Saxony-Anhalt from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007) was used.

2.2.4. Allocation rules Allocation problems arise, when the life cycles of different products are connected (Hospido et al., 2003). In this study allo- cation was used for crops being the raw material for more than one product. Furthermore allocation was applied to the three animal products milk, beef/breeding animals and manure. Inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of fodder crops and the animal metabolism were allocated to main- and co-products according to their economic value (economic allocation). The allocation ratios for the feed and the respective co-products were calculated (if not stated otherwise) from dry mass/ha and local prices: barley straw:barley grains, 44:56; wheat middling: wheat flour, 34:66; soybean cake:soybean oil, 68:32 (Van der Werf, 2004); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 13:87 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007); maize straw:maize corn:, 7:93. For the allocation between milk, beef/ breeding animals and manure the ratios 63:37 for the highlands (no manure sold; (Bartl et al., 2009)) and 96:3:1 for the coast ((Parola, 2009); raw data from the first interview) were applied. The fresh mass of the feed and their co-products was the allocation criterion for processing of the feed at mills or oil plants and transport (mass allocation). The ratios used were: wheat middling:whea flour, 23:77 (personal communication, Molinera del Centro, Lima, Peru, February 2009); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 63:37 (MINAG, 2008a); cotton seed cake:cotton seed oil, 67:33 (Naturland, 2000); soybean take:soybean oil, 80:20 (Van der Werf, 2004).

with regard to their economic situation (Parola, 2009). Data not included in these reports was collected in detailed interviews with 3 member farmers and also samples of concentrate ingredients were taken. Production inputs for the off farm cultivation and processing of the maize straw and concentrate ingredients was obtained from literature (see footnotes to Table 2). For wheat production in Argentina no information about production inputs and activities was available and due to similar yields data for conventional wheat in Saxony-Anhalt from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007) was used.

2.2.4. Allocation rules Allocation problems arise, when the life cycles of different products are connected (Hospido et al., 2003). In this study allo- cation was used for crops being the raw material for more than one product. Furthermore allocation was applied to the three animal products milk, beef/breeding animals and manure. Inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of fodder crops and the animal metabolism were allocated to main- and co-products according to their economic value (economic allocation). The allocation ratios for the feed and the respective co-products were calculated (if not stated otherwise) from dry mass/ha and local prices: barley straw:barley grains, 44:56; wheat middling: wheat flour, 34:66; soybean cake:soybean oil, 68:32 (Van der Werf, 2004); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 13:87 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007); maize straw:maize corn:, 7:93. For the allocation between milk, beef/ breeding animals and manure the ratios 63:37 for the highlands (no manure sold; (Bartl et al., 2009)) and 96:3:1 for the coast ((Parola, 2009); raw data from the first interview) were applied. The fresh mass of the feed and their co-products was the allocation criterion for processing of the feed at mills or oil plants and transport (mass allocation). The ratios used were: wheat middling:whea flour, 23:77 (personal communication, Molinera del Centro, Lima, Peru, February 2009); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 63:37 (MINAG, 2008a); cotton seed cake:cotton seed oil, 67:33 (Naturland, 2000); soybean take:soybean oil, 80:20 (Van der Werf, 2004).

6: Regional data was used whenever available as the demonstra- tion of the differences between the two systems analyzed was one of the goals of this study. Data for the highland system was collected in a survey of 26 farmers from two highland communities in the central Peruvian Andes (Chalhuas and Sallahuachac; prov- ince of Jauja, district of Sincos; published in (Bartl et al., 2009); Tables 1 and 2). For its use in the LCA annual average values were calculated. Additionally the nutrient contents of 74 samples of the most common feed were available for the highland system (Bartl et al., 2009a). Another source of data with a high regional context was information generated in a feeding trial with cattle and feed typical for the central Peruvian Andes (Bartl et al., 2009b). The production and nutritional values of forages were adopted from a growing experiment conducted in Chalhuas and Sallahuachac (Bartl et al., 2009c).

For the coastal system farm level data was obtained from three surveys of farmers in the irrigation zone San Felipe, at the coast about 135 km north of Lima (province of Huaura, district of Lima). Data concerning feeding and milk production was collected from 51 member farmers of an association of smallholder dairy producers
Fig. 2. System boundary for the coastal production system.

for the preparation of a baseline document for rural extension activities by the Universidad Nacional Agraria La Molina in Lima, Faculty of Animal Production (Gómez, 2008) (Tables 1 and 2). Another 20 farmers of the same association were interviewed twice

with regard to their economic situation (Parola, 2009). Data not included in these reports was collected in detailed interviews with 3 member farmers and also samples of concentrate ingredients were taken. Production inputs for the off farm cultivation and processing of the maize straw and concentrate ingredients was obtained from literature (see footnotes to Table 2). For wheat production in Argentina no information about production inputs and activities was available and due to similar yields data for conventional wheat in Saxony-Anhalt from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007) was used.

2.2.4. Allocation rules Allocation problems arise, when the life cycles of different products are connected (Hospido et al., 2003). In this study allo- cation was used for crops being the raw material for more than one product. Furthermore allocation was applied to the three animal products milk, beef/breeding animals and manure. Inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of fodder crops and the animal metabolism were allocated to main- and co-products according to their economic value (economic allocation). The allocation ratios for the feed and the respective co-products were calculated (if not stated otherwise) from dry mass/ha and local prices: barley straw:barley grains, 44:56; wheat middling: wheat flour, 34:66; soybean cake:soybean oil, 68:32 (Van der Werf, 2004); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 13:87 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007); maize straw:maize corn:, 7:93. For the allocation between milk, beef/ breeding animals and manure the ratios 63:37 for the highlands (no manure sold; (Bartl et al., 2009)) and 96:3:1 for the coast ((Parola, 2009); raw data from the first interview) were applied. The fresh mass of the feed and their co-products was the allocation criterion for processing of the feed at mills or oil plants and transport (mass allocation). The ratios used were: wheat middling:whea flour, 23:77 (personal communication, Molinera del Centro, Lima, Peru, February 2009); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 63:37 (MINAG, 2008a); cotton seed cake:cotton seed oil, 67:33 (Naturland, 2000); soybean take:soybean oil, 80:20 (Van der Werf, 2004).

9: Regional data was used whenever available as the demonstra- tion of the differences between the two systems analyzed was one of the goals of this study. Data for the highland system was collected in a survey of 26 farmers from two highland communities in the central Peruvian Andes (Chalhuas and Sallahuachac; prov- ince of Jauja, district of Sincos; published in (Bartl et al., 2009); Tables 1 and 2). For its use in the LCA annual average values were calculated. Additionally the nutrient contents of 74 samples of the most common feed were available for the highland system (Bartl et al., 2009a). Another source of data with a high regional context was information generated in a feeding trial with cattle and feed typical for the central Peruvian Andes (Bartl et al., 2009b). The production and nutritional values of forages were adopted from a growing experiment conducted in Chalhuas and Sallahuachac (Bartl et al., 2009c).

For the coastal system farm level data was obtained from three surveys of farmers in the irrigation zone San Felipe, at the coast about 135 km north of Lima (province of Huaura, district of Lima). Data concerning feeding and milk production was collected from 51 member farmers of an association of smallholder dairy producers
Fig. 2. System boundary for the coastal production system.

for the preparation of a baseline document for rural extension activities by the Universidad Nacional Agraria La Molina in Lima, Faculty of Animal Production (Gómez, 2008) (Tables 1 and 2). Another 20 farmers of the same association were interviewed twice

with regard to their economic situation (Parola, 2009). Data not included in these reports was collected in detailed interviews with 3 member farmers and also samples of concentrate ingredients were taken. Production inputs for the off farm cultivation and processing of the maize straw and concentrate ingredients was obtained from literature (see footnotes to Table 2). For wheat production in Argentina no information about production inputs and activities was available and due to similar yields data for conventional wheat in Saxony-Anhalt from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007) was used.

2.2.4. Allocation rules Allocation problems arise, when the life cycles of different products are connected (Hospido et al., 2003). In this study allo- cation was used for crops being the raw material for more than one product. Furthermore allocation was applied to the three animal products milk, beef/breeding animals and manure. Inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of fodder crops and the animal metabolism were allocated to main- and co-products according to their economic value (economic allocation). The allocation ratios for the feed and the respective co-products were calculated (if not stated otherwise) from dry mass/ha and local prices: barley straw:barley grains, 44:56; wheat middling: wheat flour, 34:66; soybean cake:soybean oil, 68:32 (Van der Werf, 2004); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 13:87 (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007); maize straw:maize corn:, 7:93. For the allocation between milk, beef/ breeding animals and manure the ratios 63:37 for the highlands (no manure sold; (Bartl et al., 2009)) and 96:3:1 for the coast ((Parola, 2009); raw data from the first interview) were applied. The fresh mass of the feed and their co-products was the allocation criterion for processing of the feed at mills or oil plants and transport (mass allocation). The ratios used were: wheat middling:whea flour, 23:77 (personal communication, Molinera del Centro, Lima, Peru, February 2009); cotton seeds:cotton fiber, 63:37 (MINAG, 2008a); cotton seed cake:cotton seed oil, 67:33 (Naturland, 2000); soybean take:soybean oil, 80:20 (Van der Werf, 2004).

12: Regional data was used whenever available as the demonstra- tion of the differences between the two systems analyzed was one of the goals of this study. Data for the highland system was collected in a survey of 26 farmers from two highland communities in the central Peruvian Andes (Chalhuas and Sallahuachac; prov- ince of Jauja, district of Sincos; published in (Bartl et al., 2009); Tables 1 and 2). For its use in the LCA annual average values were calculated. Additionally the nutrient contents of 74 samples of the most common feed were available for the highland system (Bartl et al., 2009a). Another source of data with a high regional context was information generated in a feeding trial with cattle and feed typical for the central Peruvian Andes (Bartl et al., 2009b). The production and nutritional values of forages were adopted from a growing experiment conducted in Chalhuas and Sallahuachac (Bartl et al., 2009c).

For the coastal system farm level data was obtained from three surveys of farmers in the irrigation zone San Felipe, at the coast about 135 km north of Lima (province of Huaura, district of Lima). Data concerning feeding and milk production was collected from 51 member farmers of an association of smallholder dairy producers

14: Resource inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of forages and concentrate ingredients, the use of pastures, manure storage, transport, processing of feed and enteric fermentation by cattle were estimated. Equations and assumptions used for the estimation of emissions from non-combustion sources to air, water and soil are summarized in Tables 3 and 4. Resource inputs for and emissions from fertilizer and pesticide production were taken from Nemecek and Kägi, 2007. Transport distances were adjusted assuming the following origins of the fertilizers: Urea, Novgorod Oblast, Russia; Diammonium phosphate and Triple superphosphate, Bone Valley, USA; Potassium sulphate, Salar de Atacama, Chile; Potassium chloride, Saskatchewan, Canada; Calcium ammonium nitrate, Argentina (due to lack of data no production region was defined, European average transport distances were used (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). For the pesticides, the average European transport distances used by Nemecek and Kägi, 2007 for the transport from the chemical plant to a local storehouse were kept unchanged due to the lack of data concerning average transport distances of

pesticides for exportation. Additionally an oversea transport of 7118 km (27% from Latin America, 25% from Peru, 20% from Europe, 9% from USA, 19% from other countries) for pesticides imported into Peru and of 7263 km (39% from Brasil, 16% from USA, 5% from China, 4% from Europe, 4% from Israel, 20% from other countries) for pesticides imported into Argentina were assumed as well as local transport distances to storehouses of 130 km in Peru and 200 km in Argentina. Assumed means of transport for fertilizers and pesticides were a transoceanic freight ship, a lorry t, fleet average Europe) and a freight train (Europe), all described in Spielmann et al., 2007. (Panichelli, 2006); grain and forage maize, (MINAG, 2008b); cotton, (MINAG, 2008c)) and resource use and emissions for these opera- tions were taken from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Electricity used in the Lima region in Peru was assumed to be produced by hydropower (58%) and combustion of natural gas (42%; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008a)). Average US values were taken for the energy production processes (Frischknecht et al., 2007). For all energy inputs for processes not taking place in Peru, the European average electricity mix from Frischknecht et al., 2007 was used. The fuel used in Peru is based on crude oil from national exploration (36%) and imports from Latin American and African countries (64%) and is refined in refineries at the Peruvian coast. Exploitation and refinery processes were taken from Jungbluth, 2007 for crude oil production in Latin America and Africa and refinery of diesel and unleaded petrol. Transport distances in producing countries were kept unchanged and sea transports of 2286 km and 13059 km from Latin America (74% from Ecuador, 12% from Brasil, 7% Columbia, 6% Venzuela; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) and Africa (88% from Angola, 12% from Nigeria; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) were assumed, respec- tively. For national production a pipeline transport distance of 827 km was considered. No transport from the harbor to the refinery was assumed as the plants are situated at the harbors and a distribution distance of 100 km (for distribution in the Lima district) of the final products was taken into account.

(Panichelli, 2006); grain and forage maize, (MINAG, 2008b); cotton, (MINAG, 2008c)) and resource use and emissions for these opera- tions were taken from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Electricity used in the Lima region in Peru was assumed to be produced by hydropower (58%) and combustion of natural gas (42%; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008a)). Average US values were taken for the energy production processes (Frischknecht et al., 2007). For all energy inputs for processes not taking place in Peru, the European average electricity mix from Frischknecht et al., 2007 was used. The fuel used in Peru is based on crude oil from national exploration (36%) and imports from Latin American and African countries (64%) and is refined in refineries at the Peruvian coast. Exploitation and refinery processes were taken from Jungbluth, 2007 for crude oil production in Latin America and Africa and refinery of diesel and unleaded petrol. Transport distances in producing countries were kept unchanged and sea transports of 2286 km and 13059 km from Latin America (74% from Ecuador, 12% from Brasil, 7% Columbia, 6% Venzuela; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) and Africa (88% from Angola, 12% from Nigeria; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) were assumed, respec- tively. For national production a pipeline transport distance of 827 km was considered. No transport from the harbor to the refinery was assumed as the plants are situated at the harbors and a distribution distance of 100 km (for distribution in the Lima district) of the final products was taken into account.
18: Resource inputs for and emissions from the cultivation of forages and concentrate ingredients, the use of pastures, manure storage, transport, processing of feed and enteric fermentation by cattle were estimated. Equations and assumptions used for the estimation of emissions from non-combustion sources to air, water and soil are summarized in Tables 3 and 4. Resource inputs for and emissions from fertilizer and pesticide production were taken from Nemecek and Kägi, 2007. Transport distances were adjusted assuming the following origins of the fertilizers: Urea, Novgorod Oblast, Russia; Diammonium phosphate and Triple superphosphate, Bone Valley, USA; Potassium sulphate, Salar de Atacama, Chile; Potassium chloride, Saskatchewan, Canada; Calcium ammonium nitrate, Argentina (due to lack of data no production region was defined, European average transport distances were used (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). For the pesticides, the average European transport distances used by Nemecek and Kägi, 2007 for the transport from the chemical plant to a local storehouse were kept unchanged due to the lack of data concerning average transport distances of

pesticides for exportation. Additionally an oversea transport of 7118 km (27% from Latin America, 25% from Peru, 20% from Europe, 9% from USA, 19% from other countries) for pesticides imported into Peru and of 7263 km (39% from Brasil, 16% from USA, 5% from China, 4% from Europe, 4% from Israel, 20% from other countries) for pesticides imported into Argentina were assumed as well as local transport distances to storehouses of 130 km in Peru and 200 km in Argentina. Assumed means of transport for fertilizers and pesticides were a transoceanic freight ship, a lorry t, fleet average Europe) and a freight train (Europe), all described in Spielmann et al., 2007. All other transport operations including transport of agricultural production inputs from a local storehouse to the dairy farm and of animal feed from the farm where they were grown to the processing plant and thereafter to the dairy farm were assumed to be realized using either a lorry 16 t (fleet average Europe), a lorry 3.5-16 t (fleet average Europe), a transport van <3.5 t or a transoceanic freight ship (for feed from Argentina; all from Spielmann et al., 2007). Type and amount of agricultural operations for the cultivation of forages and raw materials for feed were taken from literature (wheat, (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007); soybeans,

(Panichelli, 2006); grain and forage maize, (MINAG, 2008b); cotton, (MINAG, 2008c)) and resource use and emissions for these opera- tions were taken from Nemecek and Kägi (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Electricity used in the Lima region in Peru was assumed to be produced by hydropower (58%) and combustion of natural gas (42%; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008a)). Average US values were taken for the energy production processes (Frischknecht et al., 2007). For all energy inputs for processes not taking place in Peru, the European average electricity mix from Frischknecht et al., 2007 was used. The fuel used in Peru is based on crude oil from national exploration (36%) and imports from Latin American and African countries (64%) and is refined in refineries at the Peruvian coast. Exploitation and refinery processes were taken from Jungbluth, 2007 for crude oil production in Latin America and Africa and refinery of diesel and unleaded petrol. Transport distances in producing countries were kept unchanged and sea transports of 2286 km and 13059 km from Latin America (74% from Ecuador, 12% from Brasil, 7% Columbia, 6% Venzuela; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) and Africa (88% from Angola, 12% from Nigeria; (Ministerio de Energía y Minas, 2008b)) were assumed, respec- tively. For national production a pipeline transport distance of 827 km was considered. No transport from the harbor to the refinery was assumed as the plants are situated at the harbors and a distribution distance of 100 km (for distribution in the Lima district) of the final products was taken into account.
21: The impact categories global warming, acidification and eutro- phication were included in the LCA. SimaPro 7.1.8 LCA software (Consultants, 2008) was used for calculations.

The global warming potential was calculated using climate change factors of IPCC, 2007 with a timeframe of 20-years (see discussion). CO2 binding capacities of plants were excluded as C fixed in feed is normally released within a few years and sequestration is

therefore not relevant for global warming. Consequently, respiration of the animals was also omitted. For acidification and eutrophication the CML 2001 method developed by the Center of Environmental Science of Leiden University (CML) was used (Guinée et al., 2002).
22: The sensitivity of the LCIA results to allocation choices and LCIA method was tested. Two alternative allocation methods were applied for the allocation to the livestock products milk, beef/ breeding animals and solid manure. The alternative allocation criteria were the net energy for lactation (NEL) intake for the production of milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure and the amounts of gross energy released by means of the products milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure. For the allocation method based on the NEL intake the amounts of NEL used for maintenance and milk production were calculated according to ALP (ALP, 2006). A cow with 330 (highlands) or 489 (coast) kg body weight needs daily 22.7 or 30.4 MJ NEL for maintenance and 12.6 or 65.4 MJ NEL for lactation, respectively. For the production of manure, the same NEL requirements as for maintenance were assumed. For calculation of allocation values the total of NEL requirements for maintenance and milk production were allocated to the product milk and NEL requirements for maintenance to the products beef/breeding animals and manure. Based on these calculations an allocation of 61% to the product milk and of 39% to the product beef/breeding animals was applied for sensitivity analysis for the highland system. For the coastal system the ratio 61:19:19 (milk:beef/breeding ani- mals:solid manure) was used. The allocation ratio based on the gross energy released with the livestock products for the highland system was 36:64 (milk:beef) and was calculated from an annual production of 3787 kg milk/farm (2650 kJ/kg 3.5% fat corrected milk; (Fineli, 2009)) and of 953 kg beef or breeding animals/farm (30,689 kJ/kg empty body weight; (NRC, 2001)). the coastal system was 1:1:98 according to the annual production of 29,104 kg milk/farm, 1120 kg beef or breeding animals/farm and 301 t DM solid manure (17,681 kJ/kg DM solid manure; (Van Horn et al., 1994)). For sensitivity analysis also an alternative allocation method for the dairy feed and the respective co-products was applied, which was based on the NEL yields/ha of feed and co- products. The ratios used were 69:31 for maize corn:maize straw (Bartl et al., 2009a; NRC, 2001), 81:19 for wheat middling:v flour (NRC, 2001), 58:42 for soybean lake:soybean oil (RAP, 1999) and 31:69 for barley straw:barley grains (NRC, 2001). For cotton seeds and cotton fiber the gross energy contents were used for allocation as no NEL value for cotton lint was available in literature (67:33 for cotton seeds:cotton fiber; (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). Furthermore, the global warming potential was calculated using the alternative LCIA methods CML2001 (Guinée et al., 2002) and Impact 2002+ (Jolliet 2005). The latter method was also applied to the impact categories acidification and eutrophication.

the coastal system was 1:1:98 according to the annual production of 29,104 kg milk/farm, 1120 kg beef or breeding animals/farm and 301 t DM solid manure (17,681 kJ/kg DM solid manure; (Van Horn et al., 1994)). For sensitivity analysis also an alternative allocation method for the dairy feed and the respective co-products was applied, which was based on the NEL yields/ha of feed and co- products. The ratios used were 69:31 for maize corn:maize straw (Bartl et al., 2009a; NRC, 2001), 81:19 for wheat middling:v flour (NRC, 2001), 58:42 for soybean lake:soybean oil (RAP, 1999) and 31:69 for barley straw:barley grains (NRC, 2001). For cotton seeds and cotton fiber the gross energy contents were used for allocation as no NEL value for cotton lint was available in literature (67:33 for cotton seeds:cotton fiber; (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). Furthermore, the global warming potential was calculated using the alternative LCIA methods CML2001 (Guinée et al., 2002) and Impact 2002+ (Jolliet 2005). The latter method was also applied to the impact categories acidification and eutrophication.
24: The sensitivity of the LCIA results to allocation choices and LCIA method was tested. Two alternative allocation methods were applied for the allocation to the livestock products milk, beef/ breeding animals and solid manure. The alternative allocation criteria were the net energy for lactation (NEL) intake for the production of milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure and the amounts of gross energy released by means of the products milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure. For the allocation method based on the NEL intake the amounts of NEL used for maintenance and milk production were calculated according to ALP (ALP, 2006). A cow with 330 (highlands) or 489 (coast) kg body weight needs daily 22.7 or 30.4 MJ NEL for maintenance and 12.6 or 65.4 MJ NEL for lactation, respectively. For the production of manure, the same NEL requirements as for maintenance were assumed. For calculation of allocation values the total of NEL requirements for maintenance and milk production were allocated to the product milk and NEL requirements for maintenance to the products beef/breeding animals and manure. Based on these calculations an allocation of 61% to the product milk and of 39% to the product beef/breeding animals was applied for sensitivity analysis for the highland system. For the coastal system the ratio 61:19:19 (milk:beef/breeding ani- mals:solid manure) was used. The allocation ratio based on the gross energy released with the livestock products for the highland system was 36:64 (milk:beef) and was calculated from an annual production of 3787 kg milk/farm (2650 kJ/kg 3.5% fat corrected milk; (Fineli, 2009)) and of 953 kg beef or breeding animals/farm (30,689 kJ/kg empty body weight; (NRC, 2001)). the coastal system was 1:1:98 according to the annual production of 29,104 kg milk/farm, 1120 kg beef or breeding animals/farm and 301 t DM solid manure (17,681 kJ/kg DM solid manure; (Van Horn et al., 1994)). For sensitivity analysis also an alternative allocation method for the dairy feed and the respective co-products was applied, which was based on the NEL yields/ha of feed and co- products. The ratios used were 69:31 for maize corn:maize straw (Bartl et al., 2009a; NRC, 2001), 81:19 for wheat middling:v flour (NRC, 2001), 58:42 for soybean lake:soybean oil (RAP, 1999) and 31:69 for barley straw:barley grains (NRC, 2001). For cotton seeds and cotton fiber the gross energy contents were used for allocation as no NEL value for cotton lint was available in literature (67:33 for cotton seeds:cotton fiber; (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). Furthermore, the global warming potential was calculated using the alternative LCIA methods CML2001 (Guinée et al., 2002) and Impact 2002+ (Jolliet 2005). The latter method was also applied to the impact categories acidification and eutrophication.
27: The sensitivity of the LCIA results to allocation choices and LCIA method was tested. Two alternative allocation methods were applied for the allocation to the livestock products milk, beef/ breeding animals and solid manure. The alternative allocation criteria were the net energy for lactation (NEL) intake for the production of milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure and the amounts of gross energy released by means of the products milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure. For the allocation method based on the NEL intake the amounts of NEL used for maintenance and milk production were calculated according to ALP (ALP, 2006). A cow with 330 (highlands) or 489 (coast) kg body weight needs daily 22.7 or 30.4 MJ NEL for maintenance and 12.6 or 65.4 MJ NEL for lactation, respectively. For the production of manure, the same NEL requirements as for maintenance were assumed. For calculation of allocation values the total of NEL requirements for maintenance and milk production were allocated to the product milk and NEL requirements for maintenance to the products beef/breeding animals and manure. Based on these calculations an allocation of 61% to the product milk and of 39% to the product beef/breeding animals was applied for sensitivity analysis for the highland system. For the coastal system the ratio 61:19:19 (milk:beef/breeding ani- mals:solid manure) was used. The allocation ratio based on the gross energy released with the livestock products for the highland system was 36:64 (milk:beef) and was calculated from an annual production of 3787 kg milk/farm (2650 kJ/kg 3.5% fat corrected milk; (Fineli, 2009)) and of 953 kg beef or breeding animals/farm (30,689 kJ/kg empty body weight; (NRC, 2001)). the coastal system was 1:1:98 according to the annual production of 29,104 kg milk/farm, 1120 kg beef or breeding animals/farm and 301 t DM solid manure (17,681 kJ/kg DM solid manure; (Van Horn et al., 1994)). For sensitivity analysis also an alternative allocation method for the dairy feed and the respective co-products was applied, which was based on the NEL yields/ha of feed and co- products. The ratios used were 69:31 for maize corn:maize straw (Bartl et al., 2009a; NRC, 2001), 81:19 for wheat middling:v flour (NRC, 2001), 58:42 for soybean lake:soybean oil (RAP, 1999) and 31:69 for barley straw:barley grains (NRC, 2001). For cotton seeds and cotton fiber the gross energy contents were used for allocation as no NEL value for cotton lint was available in literature (67:33 for cotton seeds:cotton fiber; (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). Furthermore, the global warming potential was calculated using the alternative LCIA methods CML2001 (Guinée et al., 2002) and Impact 2002+ (Jolliet 2005). The latter method was also applied to the impact categories acidification and eutrophication.
29: The sensitivity of the LCIA results to allocation choices and LCIA method was tested. Two alternative allocation methods were applied for the allocation to the livestock products milk, beef/ breeding animals and solid manure. The alternative allocation criteria were the net energy for lactation (NEL) intake for the production of milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure and the amounts of gross energy released by means of the products milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure. For the allocation method based on the NEL intake the amounts of NEL used for maintenance and milk production were calculated according to ALP (ALP, 2006). A cow with 330 (highlands) or 489 (coast) kg body weight needs daily 22.7 or 30.4 MJ NEL for maintenance and 12.6 or 65.4 MJ NEL for lactation, respectively. For the production of manure, the same NEL requirements as for maintenance were assumed. For calculation of allocation values the total of NEL requirements for maintenance and milk production were allocated to the product milk and NEL requirements for maintenance to the products beef/breeding animals and manure. Based on these calculations an allocation of 61% to the product milk and of 39% to the product beef/breeding animals was applied for sensitivity analysis for the highland system. For the coastal system the ratio 61:19:19 (milk:beef/breeding ani- mals:solid manure) was used. The allocation ratio based on the gross energy released with the livestock products for the highland system was 36:64 (milk:beef) and was calculated from an annual production of 3787 kg milk/farm (2650 kJ/kg 3.5% fat corrected milk; (Fineli, 2009)) and of 953 kg beef or breeding animals/farm (30,689 kJ/kg empty body weight; (NRC, 2001)). the coastal system was 1:1:98 according to the annual production of 29,104 kg milk/farm, 1120 kg beef or breeding animals/farm and 301 t DM solid manure (17,681 kJ/kg DM solid manure; (Van Horn et al., 1994)). For sensitivity analysis also an alternative allocation method for the dairy feed and the respective co-products was applied, which was based on the NEL yields/ha of feed and co- products. The ratios used were 69:31 for maize corn:maize straw (Bartl et al., 2009a; NRC, 2001), 81:19 for wheat middling:v flour (NRC, 2001), 58:42 for soybean lake:soybean oil (RAP, 1999) and 31:69 for barley straw:barley grains (NRC, 2001). For cotton seeds and cotton fiber the gross energy contents were used for allocation as no NEL value for cotton lint was available in literature (67:33 for cotton seeds:cotton fiber; (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007)). Furthermore, the global warming potential was calculated using the alternative LCIA methods CML2001 (Guinée et al., 2002) and Impact 2002+ (Jolliet 2005). The latter method was also applied to the impact categories acidification and eutrophication.
32: From the local storehouse where most of the regional farmers in the coastal proyect region purchase the concentrate one sample of each concentrate ingredient was taken. The feed samples were dried at 60 °C and milled to pass a 1 m screen and thereafter analysed for DM and nitrogen (N) content according to the AOAC procedures section 950.46 and 984.13, respectively (AOAC, 1990). Crude protein was calculated as 6.25 N.
33: 3.1. Life cycle inventory for 1 kg ECM
Permanent pastures contribute to 84.5% of all land used in the highlands and 96.7% of the water is consumed for the irrigation of

forages. The land use for milk production at the coast is distributed between production of forages (58.3% of total land used) and concentrate ingredients (41.7%). Most of the water (77.8%) and energy (76.9%) used at the coast are spent for the production of the concentrate ingredients (Table 5).

39: In the highlands livestock and pastures contribute 81.7 and 15.6% to total global warming potential, respectively (Table 6). Methane accounts for 93.2% of all CO2 equivalents. At the coast enteric fermentation and manure storage contribute 73.9% of CO2 equivalents to global warming, followed by the cultivation and processing of the concentrate ingredients (7.86%) and the cultiva- tion of the forages (7.55%). Methane, NO2 and the CO2 released from soil cultivation and land transformation contributes with 92% of all CO2 equivalents to global warming. Emissions per animal are up to 200 times higher in the highlands and up to 2500 times higher at the coast than emissions per kg ECM. Whereas the global warming potential for 1 kg ECM is more than 300% higher in the highlands than at the coast, mainly due to livestock emissions, emissions for 1 animal are 180% higher at the coast than in the highlands.

For all three functional units, acidification and eutrophication in the highlands are induced mainly by the use of pastures (83.9 and 92.7% of all SO2 and PO4 equivalents, respectively; Table 7). Whereas acidification in the highlands is caused to 98.2% by NH3, the main source of eutrophication is nitrate (60.4% of all PO4 equivalents). Activities mainly responsible for acidification and

eutrophication at the coast are the cultivation and processing of the forages (46.8 and 54.3% of all SO2 and PO4 equivalents respectively) and the concentrate ingredients (21.4 and 32.9%). Gases mainly responsible for acidification are NH3 and SO2. Nitrate is the main substance causing eutrophication (59.4% of all PO4 equivalents) at the coast. For each kg ECM produced in the highlands acidification is higher by 87% and eutrophication by 220% than for 1 kg ECM from the coast. Expressed per ha and animal, emissions are more than 500% higher for acidification and more than 250% higher for eutrophication in the coastal compared to the highland system.

41: Using an alternative allocation method for allocation to the live- stock products milk, beef/breeding animals and solid manure does generally have a higher effect on the coastal than on the highland system (Table 8). Only in one case, when using the NEL contained in the livestock products as allocation criterion and applying it to the water use, the order of preference of the two systems changes, showing a lower water use for the coastal than for the highland system. Using the NEL yield as criterion for the allocation to feed and feed co-products the impacts relative to the LCIA results change by +0.3 and -10.8% for the highland and the coastal system, respectively. The order of preference of the two systems does not change for any of the impacts. The use of an alternative impact assessment method substantially more decreases the global warming

44: When interpreting the LCA results it has to be considered that due to the lack of real farm data regarding emissions, the envi- ronmental impact calculated with an LCA differs from the actual environmental impact (Thomassen et al., 2008). Especially when comparing different systems producing the same product the accuracy of data has to be high and a large amount of data has to be available (Basset-Mens and Werf, 2005). Thanks to detailed data collection for other purposes sufficient accurate on farm data was available for this study and also various literature sources offered adequate agricultural data for off farm crop production.

Emission data for agriculture in tropical mountain or desert regions are very scarce and the majority of emission models was developed for industrial state systems and temperate climates. Especially for the highland system, characterized by a very low productivity, local dual-purpose cattle types and a low quality diet emission models need to be adapted. Another bottleneck is the lack of specific life cycle inventories. This especially applies to transport, energy and agricultural production.

Furthermore it seems that common allocation methods do not allow an appropriate description of subsistence systems such as the highland system in this study. Non monetary values of system elements such as the importance of the bulls as working animals and of the cattle herd as saving account have to be considered. The use of different functional units such as kg ECM, ha and animal as applied in this study, is one possible approach reflecting the multi- functionality of a system. Further research activities will have to focus on the quantification of economic values of different func- tions of livestock in the tropics.

46: The higher occupation of land per kg ECM in the highlands compared with the coastal system is mainly due to the large area of permanent pasture used. Reason for this is the low productivity of these pastures, which allows only low stocking rates (Flores et al., 2005). Although the differences between the two systems are considerable, no comparison of the impact on the environment due to land occupation can be given. Rather the quality of land use, which includes land occupation as one of various factors, would have to be considered for this purpose (Köllner and Scholz, 2007). Nevertheless one can assume that the permanent pastures have a higher biodi- versity potential than the arable land under intensive management used for milk production at the coast. The high amount of water used for 1 kg ECM in the coastal system is determined by the use of irri- gation water for forages and concentrate ingredients. Agricultural production in the coastal regions of the provinces Ica and Huaura, which in this study were assumed to be the producing regions for cotton and maize, depends to 100% on irrigation (SENAMHI, 2009) using water from nearby rivers, lakes or underground sources.
47: The global warming potential is defined as the contribution to atmospheric absorption of infrared radiation by anthropogenically

derived gases such as CH4, CO2 and N20 leading to an increase in global temperature (Harrison, 2001). Compared to European milk production systems, which were estimated to produce on average 1.04 kg CO2 equivalents/kg ECM and 1.20 kg CO2 equivalents/kg milk (De Vries and Boer, 2010) the global warming potential calculated in this study was very high. This was mainly due to the 20-year time horizon considered for the global warming potential with a characterization factor for CH4 of 72 CO2 equivalents. Under the 100-year time horizon, used in the other studies, one unit CH4 is equivalent to 25 units CO2 (IPCC, 2007). We decided on the 20-year time horizon according to a draft from SCS (SCS, 2009) for a framework for LCA-based climate programs and registries. SCS (SCS, 2009) suggest the use of a 20-year time horizon considering the high level of uncertainty regarding climate effects within a 100-year time horizon and the assumption that conditions are changing much more rapidly than the 100-year time horizon can effectively accommodate. Using the IPCC (IPCC, 2007) method for a 100-year time horizon, the total kg of CO2 equivalents produced per kg ECM are 1.74 and 5.42 for the coastal and the highland system, respectively. In the roughage-only system of the highlands CH4 from enteric fermentation was the main contributor to global warming potential. This is in agreement with data found for a system with rain grown tropical grass pastures plus annual ryegrass by Chen et al (Chen et al., 2005). Other studies showed a high contribution of N20 of between 38 and 78% of CO2 equiva- lents for milk production systems based on grassland and concentrate (Thomassen et al., 2008; Thomassen and Boer, 2005). The low contribution of N20 to the global warming potential in our study is due to the characterization factors used which reduced the relative importance of N20 compared to other studies with a 100- year time horizon. In the coastal system soil cultivation and land transformation contributed about 11% of CO2 equivalents to global warming potential. About 45% of these emissions can be assigned to soybean cake production. Conversion of forests to cropland, which is practiced on nearly 6% of annual soybean expansion areas in Argentina (SAGPyA, 2009), releases CO2 as a result of burning or microbial decomposition of organic carbon stored in plant biomass and soils.

A low global warming potential per animal as shown for the highlands is due to the multi-functionality of the system. Small farmers opt for large herds with low productivity as keeping cattle as source of savings is a common strategy in Andean households (Valdivia and Quiroz, 2003).

50: Acidification describes the acid deposition onto soil and into water by anthropogenically derived pollutants such as SO2, NOx, NH3 and N20 (Harrison, 2001). The acidification levels calculated for 1 kg ECM are within the range reported for European regions (10-18 SO2 equivalents/kg EC (De Vries and Boer, 2010)) for the highland system but lower than these reference values for the coast. In accordance with data from other studies (Thomassen et al., 2008; Boer, 2003) NH3 emissions contribute most to the acidifica- tion potential in both systems. The major part of the NH3 is released from cattle urine and faeces directly dropped on pastures (high- lands) or in the dairy dry lots (coast) or applied on agricultural plots after storage. The total amount of NH3 volatilized per kg ECM is twice as high for the highland as for the coastal system due to the low productivity of the highland cows relative to the productivity of the cows in the coastal system. Also Boer, 2003 noted that NH3 emissions are not reduced necessarily by changing from conven- tional to organic milk production. The low value at the coast is mainly due to the fact, that the major part of the manure produced is sold and leaves the system and for the production of concentrate

ingredients and most of the forages only mineral fertilizers are used. Due to the use of vast pastures and large herds in the high- lands the acidification potentials per ha and animals are compa- rably small. Using area as functional unit the farm's function as a producer of nonmarket goods, such as environmental service, can be assessed (Van der Werf et al., 2007) and using one animal as functional unit reflects especially the capital asset function of the highland cows. Expressing the results per ha low input-low output systems have normally lower impact values than high input-high output systems (Van der Werf et al., 2007). Anyway, the sensi- tivity to acidification of the soils in the two systems seems to be low due to high base saturation levels (93% in the highland system and 100% in the coastal system; own analyses) and low cation exchange capacities, which are 34.8 me/100 for the highlands and 4.8 me/ 100 for the coast (Tao et al., 2002). Nevertheless more information about the soils in the systems is needed for sound conclusions.

52: Eutrophication results in a reduction of the oxygen concentra- tion in water or soil through provision of nutrients which increase the production of biomass (Harrison, 2001). Eutrophication per kg ECM is especially high in the highlands compared to the results of European studies (3.8-4.2 PO4 equivalents/kg (De Vries and Boer, 2010)).

Nitrate and NH3 as dominant sources of eutrophication were also found by Thomassen et al., 2008. In the coastal system, nitrate especially from the cultivation of forage maize (58.8% of all nitrate emissions) plays an important role for eutrophication. In this region of Peru all agricultural activities are dependent either on water from nearby rivers or lakes brought to the agricultural areas by means of an extensive irrigation channel system or on local underground water. The quality of the irrigation and underground water is thus supposed to be a critical issue for many coastal farmers but nevertheless little information exist about water quality in these areas. Thomassen et al., 2008 also showed a high contribution of P to eutrophication which in our study accounted for only 5.60% of all PO4 equivalents. The amount of P contamina- tion is determined by soil erosion and P content of the soil. Soil erosion in the study regions was classified to be very slight (coast) or moderate (highlands; (INRENA, 1996)) and furthermore studies of soil erosion under different types of soil use in the Venezuelan Andes associated the lowest soil loss rate among cultivated soils with pastures (Sánchez et al., 2002).

54: Also with the alternative allocation and LCIA methods the environmental burden of 1 kg ECM is for all impact categories higher in the highland than in the coastal system. Using the gross energy of the products as allocation criterion, the impacts reduce as less weight is put on milk and more on the other products. Espe- cially on the coast, where 98% of the environmental burden is allocated to manure, the impacts reduce significantly. Nevertheless, the ranking of the regions does only change for water consumption, which reduces to 38 and 436 1/kg ECM at the coast and in the highlands, respectively.

In general, when using an alternative allocation method, the highland system is less affected by changes of the environmental burden of 1 kg ECM compared to the economic allocation, due to the fact that the percentages assigned to milk by the economic and the alternative allocation methods do not differ much and barley straw, where allocation between feed and co-product is applied, accounts only for a small part of total feed intake. For the coastal system, the percentages of the environmental burden assigned to

milk and feed by the alternative allocation methods differ consid- erably from the percentages based on economic allocation. The environmental impacts derived by the different allocation methods do therefore vary considerably as well. These results might indicate that for specialized dairy systems and systems which are defined by the use of a high percentage of crop by-products as feed such as the coastal system the choice of the allocation method is a crucial point in an LCA study.

56: The environmental burden of 1 kg energy corrected milk (ECM) produced in two typical smallholder Peruvian production systems in the highlands and at the coast were shown to differ considerably with impacts being higher by 333, 87 and 219% in the highland than in the coastal system for global warming potential, acidification and eutrophication, respectively. The highland system was character- ized by a high land occupation and a high emission of methane per kg ECM. On the other hand, emissions per ha and animal were considerably smaller in the highlands than at the coast. Livestock in a LCA study related emissions contributed mainly to global warming potential, acidification and eutrophication in the highland system. In the coastal system carbon dioxide, especially from soil cultivation and land transformation, was an important output to the environment as well. Furthermore, pesticides are used only in the coastal system and water and energy consumption are 4 and more than 20,000 times higher at the coast than in the highlands. Livestock and the cultivation of the concentrate ingredients were the main sources for gases contributing to global warming and especially land transformation for soybean cultivation in Argentina accounted for a major part of CO2 emissions. Strategies in order to reduce the environmental burden of milk production should focus on increasing milk production levels and reducing methane production from enteric fermentation in the highland system. In the coastal system the environmental performance of milk production could be improved changing the concentrate compo- sition. Local ingredients should be favored and especially the exchange of imported soybean cake by another protein source should be addressed by LCA studies, in order to assure that the switch to an alternative protein source does not create other problems.

Emission data for agriculture in tropical mountain or desert regions and emission models adapted to tropical environmental and production settings, as well as specific life cycle inventories are the main bottlenecks for life cycle assessments in tropical regions and should be subject to future investigations.
